video_title: Henrik Zeberg: The Final Melt-Up Before Everything Breaks
gist: Henrik Zeberg (macro strategist, Swiss Block) argues markets are in a terminal melt-up targeting NASDAQ 33,000–34,000 and a Q3 2026 top, after which a crash combining dot-com-scale equity deflation with GFC-scale credit unwinding will unfold — while the real U.S. economy has already been deteriorating badly, masked by headline data the market is ignoring.

insights.claim: Zeberg expects at least an additional 15% gain in the NASDAQ from current levels before the cycle top, with a NASDAQ price target of 33,000–34,000 based on Fibonacci master levels and fractal analysis.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Implies remaining upside in mega-cap tech and Nasdaq-linked products (QQQ) before positioning defensively; the 33–34k target is a specific level to watch for topping signals.
insights.quote: We may see another at least 15% in the NASDAQ from from right here. And I'm not even certain that the next pullback we will see will be the one that will actually be the the big big move.

insights.claim: Zeberg calls a market top in Q3 2026, the most specific time-bounded call in the interview, based on negative momentum divergences emerging across major indices on the technical charts.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Q3 2026 is a hard deadline; investors with long equity exposure should have a risk-reduction plan by late summer 2026.
insights.quote: I'll probably get chased for this but I'll say that the top will be in Q3 of this year. And that that's being as bold as I can.

insights.claim: The NASDAQ's near-vertical rally since late March 2026 fits a 1997–2000 fractal pattern, suggesting the final parabolic phase is now underway rather than having already peaked.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: The fractal lens argues against being short today; the vertical-move phase historically accelerates before collapsing, making premature shorts expensive.
insights.quote: The vertical move is what you see into the final phase and you saw that back in 2000... we are from 97 and until 2000 that kind of development we saw there in the NASDAQ and the S&P is very much what we see right now.

insights.claim: Two confirming indicators that a top is NOT yet in: (1) liquidity conditions have not deteriorated to 2000-peak levels; (2) the S&P-to-10-year-bond ratio is still trending up rather than declining 2–3 months ahead of the peak.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Investors can use these two gauges — liquidity trends and the equity/bond ratio — as leading confirmation signals before rotating defensively.

insights.claim: The final melt-up will be characterized by accelerating capital rotation: first into U.S. large-caps and financials, then broadening into laggards like software, then crypto — each rotation signaling the cycle is aging rather than healthy.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Rotation into software and crypto should be read as a late-cycle euphoria signal, not a fundamental re-rating; it marks the window to reduce risk, not add it.

insights.claim: Zeberg was explicitly long ('bulled up') at interview time despite forecasting a recession, illustrating his core framework: a bearish macro view and a bullish market view can coexist until liquidity and technical topping signals align.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Macro bears who exited equities early have left money on the table; the framework argues for riding the melt-up until 20 specific indicators flip, not on macro fundamental data alone.
insights.quote: People always point to me and say you say that there is a recession coming... and then it comes to the market and people say but does that mean you also can bearish the market? No, it does not.

themes.theme: The Combined-Crisis Thesis: Dot-Com + GFC Simultaneously

insights.claim: Zeberg's central thesis is that the coming crash will be a hybrid: a dot-com-style equity bubble collapse (the 'appetizer') followed by a GFC-style credit/balance-sheet unwind (the 'main course'), making it worse than either crisis alone.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: A two-phase crisis means no safe equity rotation; value stocks that survived 2000 were destroyed in 2008. Investors need to plan for both equity and credit dislocation.
insights.quote: I think the .com will be the appetizer and then you'll see the 2008-9 as the main course.

insights.claim: Current equity valuations are roughly 2.5x the bubble level seen in 2007 by Zeberg's measures, making today's stock market a much larger bubble than the one that preceded the GFC.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: A larger starting-point overvaluation implies a larger deleveraging; historical mean-reversion math suggests peak-to-trough drawdowns could significantly exceed 2008's ~57%.
insights.quote: The stock market... it's a bigger bubble much much bigger like like two and a half times the size of what we saw in '07.

insights.claim: Private credit markets are flagged as the structural fault line that turns a 2000-style equity crash into a 2008-style credit crisis, analogous to the role subprime/real estate played in the GFC.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Monitor private credit stress indicators (spreads, redemption gates, NAV marks) as early warning signals of the second phase of the crisis.
insights.quote: We could see a huge deflationary factor from the fact that the stock market could implode, which I think it will, which will then cause a private credit situation.

insights.claim: The post-crash credit crunch will develop because inflated corporate valuations serve as collateral; once equities collapse in a recession, the credit underpinning those valuations is pulled and a lending freeze begins.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Corporate bond holders and leveraged-loan investors face a double whammy: equity collateral devalues while recession hits cash flows simultaneously.

insights.claim: The initial crash will last roughly 6–9 months, followed by a Fed-driven bounce of 4–5 months (repeating the Jan–May 2008 pattern), before the second and more destructive leg down as credit dominoes fall.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: The Fed-induced bounce will be a trap; selling into that relief rally rather than buying it is the key tactical call for the crisis period.
insights.quote: There will be a reaction from the Fed... and the market bounced for four or five months in 2008 as well even though we were in a recession and the markets were you know doomed.

themes.theme: U.S. Labour Market Deterioration Hidden by Headline Data

insights.claim: The August 2025 non-farm payroll revision erased approximately 1 million previously reported jobs, revealing that full-year 2025 job creation was only 184,000 — compared to ~1.5 million per year considered acceptable.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Headline NFP readings are structurally overstated; investors relying on them to gauge consumer health are making decisions on fictitious data.
insights.quote: There came you know August the revision in the non-farm payrolls and we saw that there was a 1 million jobs all of a sudden disappearing and the job creation throughout 25 was 184,000.

insights.claim: The last five months of 2025 (after revision) showed an average of minus 9,000 jobs per month; the current underlying pace Zeberg tracks is approximately 75,000–80,000 net new jobs per month — a recessionary rate historically.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: At 75–80k/month, the jobs market is already printing recession-level numbers that the Fed and markets are ignoring — a catalyst for a sharp policy U-turn.

insights.claim: May 2026 saw a loss of 79,000 full-time jobs; since January 2025, 1.7 million full-time jobs have been lost in the U.S. — masked because the headline NFP counts part-time and full-time jobs together.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Full-time job losses at this scale historically precede consumer spending contractions; watch personal consumption and retail sales for confirming weakness.
insights.quote: What we actually saw in May was that there was a loss of 79,000 full-time jobs in that same month. And since January of 25, we have seen a loss of 1.7 million jobs, full-time jobs in the US.

insights.claim: Average unemployment duration has risen to 25 weeks, versus 16 weeks going into the 2008 financial crisis — more than 50% higher, indicating the labour market is more structurally impaired than it was at the GFC onset.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Extended unemployment duration suppresses consumer spending power and confidence even before a headline recession is declared; the consumer is already weakening.
insights.quote: The duration of people's unemployment is now up to 25 weeks where before going into the financial crisis it was at 16 weeks. So we are you know more than 50% above that.

insights.claim: The job-creation-to-labour-force ratio is currently at a level only seen during recessions over the past 60 years, even using the optimistic headline NFP figures.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: A recession is already likely underway in the real economy; markets trading at peak valuations on the assumption of economic strength are mispriced.

insights.claim: Zeberg argues the bottom 80–90% of U.S. consumers may be in the worst financial shape ever recorded — worse than during the GFC or even 1929 — driven by housing unaffordability, prolonged real income erosion, and fear of job loss.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Consumer discretionary and housing-exposed sectors face a structural demand cliff, not a cyclical soft patch; CAPE-style mean reversion in consumer stocks could be severe.
insights.quote: The consumer on an aggregated level... the bottom 90%... you will see that they probably haven't been in a worse situation ever. I mean, even going in and being in the financial crisis and even also in the 1929.

insights.claim: Housing unaffordability is flagged as today's structural analogue to the 2008 real estate bubble — people can't afford to stay in their homes — but today's problem is on the demand/income side rather than levered speculation, making it harder to fix with rate cuts.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Rate cuts alone won't reflate housing if consumer incomes are falling; homebuilders and mortgage REITs face a scenario where the policy antidote fails to work.

insights.claim: U.S. national savings rate is currently 2–3%, versus 10–20% in the 1970s stagflation era — this structural difference means consumers cannot sustain spending through an inflationary period the way they did in the 70s.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: A 70s-style stagflation repeat is not the base case; instead expect deflationary demand collapse once consumers exhaust their ability to spend.
insights.quote: In the 70s people's savings rate were like 10 to 20%. Even when it was bad now it's down to 2 to 3%.

insights.claim: Zeberg's implied framework: when the post-crash fiscal/monetary stimulus arrives, consumers will SAVE rather than spend it due to scarring from prolonged financial stress — this risks a depression-length recovery, not a V-shape.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Velocity-of-money dynamics will be crucial; if stimulus does not reach the real economy, liquidity injections will inflate financial assets again without restoring GDP growth — stagflationary asset bubble redux.
insights.quote: This time if the money doesn't really start to rotate in the real economy but rotates in the financial world... then you can start to see inflation picking up while the economy is still sluggish.

themes.theme: Fed Policy, Inflation Narrative, and Regime Shift

insights.claim: Zeberg argues Powell is dramatically behind the curve; the labour market data alone justifies aggressive cuts, and Trump's pressure for a leadership change to Kevin Warsh could itself be a market catalyst that sends equities sharply higher in the near term.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: A Fed chair transition to a more dovish/accommodative leader is a plausible near-term upside catalyst; watch Fed Chair succession news as a potential melt-up accelerant.
insights.quote: One of the things that can be sending the things much higher very sharply here could be that there will actually be a regime shift from Kevin Wars.

insights.claim: High bond yields today are NOT primarily driven by inflation — they reflect late-cycle capital demand from corporates deploying heavy capex — a distinction the market is misreading as a structural inflation problem.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: If yields are a capex/demand phenomenon rather than an inflation signal, they should fall sharply when corporate investment turns down — creating a bond rally opportunity the bond market is not pricing.
insights.quote: What we see right now is that there is a big demand for capital coming from a lot of large firms putting a lot of capital in expenditures out there... this is the demand for capital late cycle phenomenon.

insights.claim: Inflation dynamics today are the opposite of the 1970s: the 70s had strong job creation (5–6x better ratio than today) and high savings to sustain price increases; today's economy lacks both, meaning persistent high inflation is not the base case.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Stagflation bears are wrong on inflation persistence; the disinflationary/deflationary consumer demand collapse is the more likely outcome, making long-duration Treasuries attractive post-crash.
insights.quote: In the 70s the non-farm payrolls the job creation was like five six end times at sometimes better ratio wise than what we are right now. That's creates that's the vehicle for inflation.

insights.claim: Zeberg argues the world has entered a new structurally higher inflation regime since 2020 (visible in three distinct inflation waves over the last decade on his charts), meaning long-term investors face a secular inflation risk even if near-term deflation hits first.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: The sequence matters: (1) buy long bonds during the deflationary crash, (2) rotate to inflation-protecting real assets as the structural inflation regime reasserts — timing the pivot is the key portfolio challenge.
insights.quote: We have now an higher inflation level it may come down it may drop but we'll not see new lows and from here on we probably see it going up.

insights.claim: During the GFC, inflation was running at 5–7% as late as mid-2008 and the Fed was cutting aggressively — the current Fed paralysis over inflation while the labour market deteriorates repeats this same historical error.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: The Fed cutting despite elevated headline inflation is historically normal in late-cycle stress; when cuts come, interpret them as recession confirmation rather than reflation.
insights.quote: Going into 2008, inflation was shooting up to 5 6 or 7% in mid 2008 and the Fed was cutting like crazy. Nobody knew we had a recession back then.

themes.theme: Crypto, Gold & Silver in the Melt-Up and Crash

insights.claim: Bitcoin and crypto will join the melt-up in a late rotation wave — after equities have already run — and will serve as the 'fat lady singing' signal that the cycle top is imminent. Zeberg has positions in Digital Asset Treasuries (DATs).
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Crypto surging to new highs is not an independent bullish signal — it marks the final speculative mania phase and is a timing indicator to begin reducing ALL risk exposure.
insights.quote: When we start to see that these extreme risk assets which they cry including Bitcoin starting to take off then you'll see that that's the fat lady singing into this late phase of the cycle.

insights.claim: Zeberg sees Bitcoin reaching $100,000–$120,000 as the cycle top range, after possibly dipping a further $3,000–$4,000 first; he does NOT expect a new all-time high above the previous peak, suggesting the $120k area would be historically bounded.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: $100k–$120k is the take-profit zone for Bitcoin longs; above that, risk/reward flips sharply negative heading into a combined equity+crypto top.
insights.quote: 100,000, yeah, 100,000 I see definitely and I think it could go to either 100 or up to 120,000. But that I think was going to be the top in that range.

insights.claim: Ethereum and altcoins will outperform Bitcoin in the final crypto leg as capital seeks higher beta within crypto — the ETH/BTC ratio historically turns up in this phase — making Ethereum a better late-cycle trade than Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Favour ETH over BTC for the late melt-up trade; when BTC tops, ETH and alts may continue briefly but then collapse faster.
insights.quote: You'll probably going to take off first and then you see Ethereum taking over which we normally see in that phase.

insights.claim: Gold and silver have already had their run — silver hit an 'Eiffel Tower' parabola (referenced around $130) and has already reversed — and will NOT make new all-time highs in this cycle.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Gold and silver longs should be cautious; the catalyst narrative (inflation hedge) has played out. A modest dollar-weakness-driven bounce is possible (DXY to ~94) but not a new high.
insights.quote: Silver is mainly a industrial product and it went to 130 or something and it was so clear that that was the same kind of Eiffel Tower move... they had their time in the sun.

insights.claim: During the crash phase, gold, silver, Bitcoin and all risk assets will sell off simultaneously as the dollar surges — forced selling to meet margin calls and loan repayments will overwhelm any 'safe haven' narrative for gold.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Do not use gold or crypto as crash hedges; the only true safe haven in the initial crisis phase will be USD cash and short-term U.S. Treasuries.
insights.quote: You actually saw that both gold and silver also went down because there was a need for cash... You need to sell it to get dollars to do it with.

insights.claim: The DXY could rally to 120 during the crash — a level not seen since ~2001 — as global dollar-short positions unwind; Zeberg compares it to 2022's rally to 114 but expects this move to be even more violent.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: A DXY-120 scenario is catastrophic for EM currencies, commodity prices, and foreign-currency-denominated U.S. corporate debt; it amplifies the deflationary shock globally.
insights.quote: I see the dollar actually heading up north of to 120 on the Dixie. And I know this sounds insane. We were at 114 I think in 22.

themes.theme: Safe Haven Positioning and Debt Dynamics

insights.claim: The only safe haven in the initial crash phase is short-duration U.S. Treasuries and USD cash; after the Fed moves to full accommodation, the safe haven trade rotates to inflation-protected real assets as the structural inflation regime reasserts.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: A barbell strategy — short Treasuries now, then rotate to real assets after the crash bottom — is the implied playbook, with the transition point triggered by aggressive Fed easing.
insights.quote: In that environment, the safe haven will be bonds in that particular period. But the moment the Fed comes out and start to say okay now we push on for real I'll be cautious because we have entered a new inflationary period.

insights.claim: U.S. federal debt-to-GDP is approximately 120% versus ~60% at the time of the GFC, meaning the government's capacity to absorb the next recession via fiscal stimulus is substantially constrained compared to 2008.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Fiscal stimulus will be limited in size relative to the problem; the recovery will be slower and more reliant on monetary policy, which has diminishing real-economy effectiveness.
insights.quote: The debt level in the US was 60% back in in 2008. Now it's 120 something on the debt to GDP.

insights.claim: Buffett's current behaviour of building cash and short-duration bond exposure is cited as corroborating evidence for the defensive crash playbook; Zeberg implies Buffett is 'staging up' for the crisis window.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Buffett's cash pile is a real-time signal aligned with Zeberg's framework; monitor Berkshire's cash deployment as an independent topping indicator.
insights.quote: I think that's what Mr. Buffett is also trying to starting to do now is, you know, staging up on on that side.

insights.claim: Capital borrowed from future growth via repeated interest rate suppression has created a structural debt overhang that guarantees the coming recession will be unusually deep; there is no painless exit.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: There is no soft-landing exit from this cycle; the debt overhang means that a growth slowdown mechanically triggers a solvency/liquidity crisis faster than in prior cycles.
insights.quote: We have been pushing and been craving tomorrow's growth by pushing the inflation in sorry the interest rates lower. We are borrowing consumption from tomorrow.

insights.claim: The 'stagnation but not stagflation' distinction is important: true stagflation requires 6–8% inflation AND 7–8% unemployment simultaneously; Zeberg believes current conditions do not qualify and the immediate risk is deflationary collapse, not stagflation.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Investors positioned for stagflation (commodity longs, TIPS) may be in the wrong trade for the near term; the deflationary crash scenario argues for bonds and USD over commodities.
insights.quote: Stagflation is when you have six, seven, eight% inflation and unemployment is staying at 7 8% as well. Then you got stagflation... Don't even use that word here. That's wrong.

themes.theme: AI's Labour Impact and Demand Destruction

insights.claim: AI's near-term net employment effect will be negative: productivity gains allow companies to produce the same output with fewer workers, and the quarter-to-quarter cost-cutting incentive ensures headcount reductions precede any new job creation by years.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: AI-driven productivity gains are a demand-destruction force in the near term; labour-intensive sectors and consumer spending will face more pressure than AI bulls are pricing in.
insights.quote: The net effect of using AI in your business would be probably that you're going to make yourself more productive and more productive people means that you lead less people.

insights.claim: AI may create jobs over a 5–10 year horizon, but the transition gap (today through ~2030) is a period of net job destruction, coinciding with the peak-bubble period Zeberg is calling for — compounding economic weakness.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: The timing overlap of AI displacement and a credit/equity crisis means the social and political response will be unusually intense, likely driving large fiscal interventions that ultimately fuel the structural inflation Zeberg warns about long-term.

insights.claim: The Hang Seng and Kospi (Samsung) are flagged as already at or near cycle tops; capital is rotating out of Asian equities into U.S. assets, which is one force fuelling the final U.S. melt-up.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: Asian equity outperformance may be exhausted; the trade is to short Asian indices on bounces while the U.S. captures the final wave of global capital rotation.
insights.quote: I think there is a top already in the in the Hang Seng for instance... and that can potentially then unfuel as I said the market in the US.

insights.claim: The SpaceX IPO — valued at over $1 trillion despite cumulative losses of $41 billion over 5–6 years and a $5 billion loss last year alone — is cited by Zeberg as the archetypal 'crazy phase' signal equivalent to the most egregious dot-com-era valuations.
insights.speaker: Henrik Zeberg
insights.so_what: The SpaceX IPO is a sentiment indicator, not an investment opportunity at current pricing; the willingness of capital to defend trillion-dollar loss-making ventures marks the psychological peak of the cycle.
insights.quote: When people try to defend an IPO that where we see a company coming out with you know having made a loss of five you know billion last year and accumulated a loss of 41 billion over the last five six years and they try to defend that the worth of that is more than trillion... that's absurd.
